title: Scientists Invent a Way to Brew Espresso With Ultrasonic Waves—No Hot Water Required
gist: Researchers at the University of New South Wales developed an ultrasonic espresso brewing method using acoustic cavitation to extract coffee compounds at room temperature, consuming 76% less energy than conventional espresso machines. Blind taste tests with 100 participants showed no statistically significant preference between ultrasonic and conventional espresso. The research, published in the Journal of Food Engineering, was led by Colombian researcher Francisco Trujillo and opens a path toward multi-format coffee makers using a single ultrasonic platform.

insights.claim: A University of New South Wales team demonstrated that espresso brewed with 100W ultrasonic technology at room temperature uses only 24% of the energy consumed by a conventional espresso machine to produce beverages of equivalent intensity—a 76% energy reduction.
insights.speaker: Javier Carbajal
insights.so_what: At commercial/industrial scale (ready-to-drink coffee production), a 76% energy reduction is a major cost and ESG advantage; watch for licensing or acquisition interest from Nestlé, JDE Peet's, or major RTD coffee producers.
insights.quote: the ultrasonic system used just 24 percent of the energy consumed by a typical espresso machine.

insights.claim: Ultrasonic espresso brewing takes approximately 2.5-3 minutes versus 30 seconds for conventional espresso—a 5-6x longer brew time that limits consumer applicability but may be acceptable for industrial batch production of ready-to-drink products.
insights.speaker: Javier Carbajal
insights.so_what: The 3-minute brew time is a barrier for café or home use but not for RTD manufacturing; industrial coffee players are the natural first market, not consumer appliance brands.
insights.quote: Although the process takes longer than the conventional method (three minutes versus 30 seconds), it consumes 75 percent less energy

insights.claim: A 100-person blind sensory panel found no significant preference between ultrasonic and conventional espresso across aroma, flavor, bitterness, and overall acceptance—and participants actually preferred the ultrasonic version for filtered coffee, rating its bitterness as more pleasant.
insights.speaker: Javier Carbajal
insights.so_what: Consumer-facing taste parity removes the most obvious commercial objection to the technology; it no longer has to compete as a 'lesser' product, clearing a major path to commercialization.
insights.quote: the participants showed no notable preference for either method. The scores for aroma, flavor, bitterness, and overall acceptance were virtually equivalent.

insights.claim: Chemical analysis confirmed that ultrasonic espresso achieves caffeine and chlorogenic acid concentrations, pH levels, and volatile aromatic compound profiles equivalent to conventionally brewed espresso, meeting Specialty Coffee Association standards for dissolved solids and extraction yield.
insights.speaker: Javier Carbajal
insights.so_what: Achieving SCA benchmark standards is the industry threshold for specialty market acceptance; meeting it unlocks premium café and RTD segments, not just commodity applications.
insights.quote: The concentrations of caffeine and chlorogenic acid were similar to those obtained by conventional methods. No significant differences were observed in pH or in the overall composition of the volatile compounds responsible for the aroma.

insights.claim: The UNSW researchers envision a single ultrasonic device capable of producing espresso, filtered coffee, and cold brew—replacing three separate machine categories with one platform, which would represent a significant appliance category consolidation opportunity.
insights.speaker: Javier Carbajal
insights.so_what: A format-agnostic brewing platform could disrupt the segmented coffee appliance market dominated by De'Longhi, Breville, and Keurig; monitor for patent filings and commercialization partnerships.
insights.quote: The research opens up the possibility of developing new coffee makers capable of preparing everything from espresso to filtered coffee and cold brew using the same technology.

insights.claim: The ultrasonic espresso system uses acoustic cavitation—the formation and collapse of tiny bubbles that generate microcurrents—to replace thermal energy as the extraction mechanism, transforming the filter basket into an 'acoustic reactor' that hits multiple points simultaneously.
insights.speaker: Javier Carbajal
insights.so_what: Acoustic cavitation is an established food science technique already used in ultrasonic cleaning and emulsification; industrial transfer risk is lower than a wholly novel technology, accelerating time-to-market.
insights.quote: Ultrasound helps us replace heat with mechanical energy
